---
title: "Approaches"
description: "Two ways to work with EpiAware — composing distributions into event trees, or assembling a full infection model from interchangeable parts."
toc: false
---

The EpiAware ecosystem supports two complementary ways of thinking about
infectious disease data. Both work with the same underlying packages, and they
can be combined in a single analysis.

::: {.approach-cards style="display: grid; grid-template-columns: 1fr 1fr; gap: 2rem; margin: 2rem 0;"}

::: {.approach-card style="border: 1px solid var(--border-color, #e6ebf0); border-radius: 12px; padding: 2rem; background: var(--bs-card-bg, #fbfcfd);"}

### Compose distributions into event trees

Model a case's progression as a tree of delays — incubation, admission,
competing outcomes — using `Sequential`, `Parallel`, `Resolve`, and `Compete`
from ComposedDistributions.jl. One object simulates and scores a record.

```{=html}
<a href="composed-distributions.qmd" class="button btn-lg button--fill" style="display: inline-block; margin-top: 1rem;">Explore &rarr;</a>
```

:::

::: {.approach-card style="border: 1px solid var(--border-color, #e6ebf0); border-radius: 12px; padding: 2rem; background: var(--bs-card-bg, #fbfcfd);"}

### Assemble an infectious disease model

Build a joint model from a latent process, an infection mechanism, and
observation layers — all interchangeable parts that plug together through the
single `as_turing_model` interface provided by ComposableTuringIDModels.jl.

```{=html}
<a href="assembled-model.qmd" class="button btn-lg button--fill" style="display: inline-block; margin-top: 1rem;">Explore &rarr;</a>
```

:::

:::
